title: Antiferromagnetic Quantum Criticality in Infinite-Layer Cuprates Sr1-xNdxCuO2
authors: Bin-Jie Wu, Ze-Xian Deng, Guang-Ran Wei, Xi Zhou, Ji-Hai Zhang, Da-Zhuang Deng, Qun Zhu, Yong Zhong, Can-Li Song, Xu-Cun Ma, Qi-Kun Xue
abstract: The interplay between quantum criticality and Fermi surface reconstruction is central to elucidating the phase diagram of high-temperature cuprate superconductors. While studies on electron-doped T'-structure cuprates suggest an antiferromagnetic origin of this reconstruction, quantitative consensus has been hindered by apical oxygen instabilities and uncontrolled oxygen vacancies. Here, we overcome these limitations by utilizing ozone-assisted molecular beam epitaxy to synthesize high-quality, oxygen-stoichiometric thin films of infinite-layer cuprate Sr1-xNdxCuO2 across its entire superconducting dome. Hall transport measurements reveal a sharp carrier-type transition signaling a Fermi surface reconstruction at a critical doping xc ~ 0.155. We show that a spin-density-wave tight-binding model quantitatively reproduces the transport evolution, supporting an antiferromagnetic origin of this quantum phase transition. Furthermore, upon suppressing superconductivity with magnetic fields, the normal-state resistivity exhibits a pristine strange metal behavior that persists down to 2 K in the vicinity of xc. Our findings establish an intrinsic, universal antiferromagnetic quantum criticality in electron-doped cuprates, positioning the structurally simplest infinite-layer cuprates as a clean benchmark platform for theories of unconventional superconductivity.
summary: This work investigates quantum criticality in electron-doped cuprates using high-quality epitaxial films of $	ext{Sr}_{1-x}	ext{Nd}_x	ext{CuO}_2$. By measuring transport properties, the authors identify a doping-dependent Fermi surface reconstruction driven by antiferromagnetic correlations. The findings establish a clean benchmark for understanding unconventional superconductivity near a quantum critical point.
main_problem: The paper aims to elucidate the phase diagram of high-temperature cuprate superconductors by understanding the interplay between quantum criticality and Fermi surface reconstruction (FSR).
main_result: The study establishes intrinsic, universal antiferromagnetic quantum criticality in electron-doped cuprates, supported by evidence of FSR and strange metal behavior near the critical doping $x_c$.
method: The research combines high-quality epitaxial thin film synthesis (ozone-assisted MBE) with detailed transport measurements (Hall effect, resistivity) and theoretical modeling using a spin-density-wave tight-binding approach.
model_or_system: The physical system is the infinite-layer cuprate $	ext{Sr}_{1-x}	ext{Nd}_x	ext{CuO}_2$. The theoretical model involves a tight-binding Hamiltonian incorporating a doping-dependent SDW gap, allowing for quantitative reproduction of the observed electronic structure evolution.
key_observables: Hall coefficient ($R_H$) sign change (electron to hole), Fermi surface reconstruction at $x_c \approx 0.155$, and linear-in-temperature/field normal-state resistivity (strange metal behavior).
important_parameters: Critical doping $x_c \approx 0.155$, temperature range down to 2 K, and the doping dependence of the SDW gap $\Delta(x)$.
main_assumptions: The infinite-layer structure is treated as a 'clean benchmark platform' because it minimizes structural instabilities present in other cuprate types.
figures_summary: Figures show XRD patterns, temperature dependence of resistivity ($ho_{xx}$), and Hall measurements illustrating the carrier-type transition and the reconstruction of the Fermi surface topology across the doping range.
paper_structure: The paper systematically presents experimental characterization of high-quality films, followed by detailed analysis of transport properties (Hall and resistivity) across the doping dome, culminating in a theoretical model that links the observed transport anomalies to an antiferromagnetic quantum critical point.
why_interesting: The strong focus on unconventional superconductivity, quantum criticality, and correlated electron physics makes this highly relevant to theoretical physicists studying strongly correlated materials and non-Fermi liquid behavior.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
